METHODS: This literature-based review evaluates five underutilized legumes in SSAâ€”African yam bean, Bambara groundnut, Kerstingâ€™s groundnut, Lima bean, and Jack beanâ€”highlighting their agronomic potential, nutritional value, and opportunities for commercialization and genetic improvement. It discusses production constraints, value chains, and the use of modern biotechnological tools like omics and genome editing.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article frames climate change as a major threat to staple crops and positions orphan legumes as climate-smart alternatives with resilience traits like drought tolerance and soil adaptability.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Orphan legumes offer untapped potential for food security, income, and climate resilience.

Lack of investment, hard-to-cook traits, poor awareness, and absence from trade statistics are barriers.

IITA and partners are conserving genetic resources and promoting advanced breeding tools.

Local markets are growing, but international visibility and formal seed systems remain lacking.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Inadequate policy attention, funding, and research focus on orphan legumes

Lack of production and consumption data

Processing and storage infrastructure are major limitations to scaling
NOTES:
Highlights IITAâ€™s leadership in seed conservation and genomics

Aligns with SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) and 13 (Climate Action)

Strong argument for re-centering neglected legumes in food systems

TITLE: Integrating temperature-dependent life table data into Insect Life Cycle Model for predicting the potential distribution of Scapsipedus icipe Hugel & Tanga
AUTHORS: Magara H.J. Otieno, Monica A. Ayieko, Saliou Niassy, Daisy Salifu, Azrag G.A. Abdelmutalab, Khamis M. Fathiya, Sevgan Subramanian, Komi K.M. Fiaboe, Nana Roos, Sunday Ekesi, Chrysantus M. Tanga
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Africa â€“ with model simulations for Eastern, Western, Central, and parts of Southern Africa
METHODS: The study developed and validated temperature-dependent demographic models for Scapsipedus icipe, an edible cricket species, using life table parameters measured at 9 constant temperatures. The models were integrated into the Insect Life Cycle Modeling (ILCYM) platform to project spatial distribution across Africa under current and future climate scenarios. Statistical analyses included ANOVA, regression modeling, and spatial simulations using WorldClim data and IPCC climate projections.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is explicitly integrated into modeling scenarios using 2050 projections, guiding adaptive food system development for insect-based protein.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Optimal growth of S. icipe occurs at 30Â°C; development fails at 10Â°C and 40Â°C.

Highest fecundity and population growth were recorded at moderate temperatures, offering clear insights for production system design.

Spatial simulations predict broad potential for establishment across Sub-Saharan Africa.

Introduces life-cycle-based conservation mapping for new edible species.

Demonstrates the utility of ILCYM in informing insect production and climate adaptation planning.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Based on constant temperature regimes; real-world variability not captured

Focused only on thermal factors, excluding humidity and resource limitations

No socioeconomic data on production feasibility or consumer acceptance
NOTES:
Funded by Danida, IDRC, ACIAR, and BioInnovate through icipe

Provides a replicable modeling approach for other new insect species

Suggests insect farming as part of African climate resilience and nutrition strategies

TITLE: Genomic Analysis of Selected Maize Landraces from Sahel and Coastal West Africa Reveals Their Variability and Potential for Genetic Enhancement
AUTHORS: Charles Nelimor, Baffour Badu-Apraku, Ana LuÃ­sa Garcia-Oliveira, Antonia Tetteh, Agre Paterne, Assanvo Simon-Pierre Nâ€™guetta, Melaku Gedil
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West Africa (Burkina Faso, Ghana, Togo)
METHODS:
Genotyped 208 maize accessions (190 landraces and 18 reference populations) using 5974 high-quality SNP markers from DArTseq.

Diversity indices (He, Ho, PIC, MAF) were estimated using PowerMarker.

Population structure was inferred using STRUCTURE (K = 1 to 12), supported by DAPC, PCoA, and NJ tree clustering.

AMOVA partitioned genetic variation within and between populations; FST and Nm were calculated for differentiation and gene flow.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article frames landrace diversity as essential for developing maize that can withstand drought and heat, both of which are increasing due to climate changeâ€”emphasizing adaptation but not mitigation.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Three distinct gene pools identified; Burkinabe gene pool was the most genetically differentiated.

High within-population variation (86%) and moderate among-population variation (14%).

Landraces show strong potential for improving stress tolerance in maize.

Reference populations were genetically similar to Ghanaian and Togolese landraces, but not to Burkinabe.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Possible misclassification of old improved varieties as landraces

Lack of eco-geographical data for many accessions

Genetic admixture due to historical germplasm exchange

Sampling bias due to historical collection methods
NOTES:
Suggests Burkinabe gene pool as a priority source for heat and drought tolerance

Supports future GWAS and marker-assisted selection

Data deposited at IITA repository and supported by the STMA project and WASCAL

TITLE: Exploring farmerâ€™s assessment of soil quality and root yield in cassava-based cropping systems
AUTHORS: Samuel Ayodele Mesele, Paul Sobowale Soremi, James Kehinde Adigun
JOURNAL: Journal of the Saudi Society of Agricultural Sciences
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Southwestern Nigeria (Ogun State, specifically Wasimi village)
METHODS: The study used participatory methods (interviews, group discussions, field observations) with 40 cassava farmers to explore indigenous indicators of soil quality and yield estimation. It compared farmersâ€™ yield predictions with actual harvest data and conducted laboratory soil analyses of four representative farm plots. A linear regression was used to compare predicted vs. measured yield.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Farmers attribute yield decline primarily to reduced rainfall. Adaptive practices include early planting and crop diversification.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Farmers use soil colour, drainage, and depth to assess land suitability

Visual yield indicators include soil cracks and leaf/stem appearance

Cassava yield has declined 10â€“50% over the past decade due to low rainfall and poor soils

Farmersâ€™ yield estimates moderately correlate with actual yields (RÂ² = 0.62)

Soil analyses confirmed low fertility across study sites

Integrating traditional knowledge with scientific metrics can strengthen agricultural interventions
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Weather data were unavailable to validate rainfall-related yield trends

Yield estimation accuracy varied widely among farmers

Soil data were limited to four farm sites
NOTES:
Strong emphasis on farmer inclusion and participatory research

No external funding was received for the research

Study underscores missed opportunities when farmer knowledge is excluded from intervention design

TITLE: Framework for Incorporating Gender Equality and Social Inclusion (GESI) Elements in Climate Information Services (CIS)
AUTHORS: Everisto Mapedza, Sophia Huyer, Nitya Chanana, Alison Rose, Inga Jacobs-Mata, Netsayi Noris Mudege, Sabine Homann-Kee Tui, Sika Gbegbelegbe, Gloriose Nsengiyumva, Munyaradzi Mutenje, Ngowenani Nohayi
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Zambia (with reference to Ethiopia, Ghana, Kenya, Mali, and Senegal under AICCRA)
METHODS: The paper uses a literature review approach to propose a gender equality and social inclusion (GESI) framework for Climate Information Services (CIS), grounded in the ongoing AICCRA project. The framework is structured around five core indicators: gender targeting by design, sex-disaggregated data collection and analysis, dissemination of technology, and continuous gender monitoring and empowerment evaluation.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate variability and its disproportionate impacts on women are the foundation for the GESI-CIS framework. The goal is to make adaptation tools more accessible and equitable.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Women are systematically excluded from CIS due to socio-cultural, institutional, and systemic barriers

Gendered access to communication technologies is critical for effective CIS delivery

Framework operationalized in Zambia via agricultural data hubs, flood/drought indicators, and training modules

Five indicators proposed to track inclusion: targeting, data, analysis, dissemination, and empowerment evaluation

Emphasizes transformative change beyond inclusionâ€”targeting structural gender norms
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
CIS alone is insufficient to empower womenâ€”norms and structural inequalities must also be addressed

Context-specific challenges in each country require flexible framework adaptation
NOTES:
Funded by the World Bank via AICCRA (P173398)

Builds directly on CGIAR-CCAFS legacy research

Highlights digital inequality and the intersectionality of gender with age, class, literacy, and disability

TITLE: Interactive effects of altitude, microclimate and shading system on coffee leaf rust
AUTHORS: Theresa Liebig, Fabienne Ribeyre, Peter LÃ¤derach, Hans-Michael Poehling, Piet van Asten, Jacques Avelino
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Uganda (Mt. Elgon region: Bulambuli, Kapchorwa, Sironko districts)
METHODS: This study examined how altitude and coffee shading systems influence the microclimate and Coffee Leaf Rust (CLR) incidence in Arabica coffee agroecosystems in Uganda. Field data (e.g., leaf rust incidence, temperature, RH, dew point) were collected over a growing season across 49 plots at three altitudes and with varying shade configurations. The authors used piecewise structural equation modeling (SEM) to assess causal relationships between environmental factors, shading systems, microclimate, and CLR development.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong climate-relevance: connects microclimatic variation across altitudes and seasons with Coffee Leaf Rust severity. Climate change implications for disease outbreaks are implied.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
CLR incidence is significantly influenced by altitude, shade system, and microclimate (e.g., dew point, night temperature, RH)

Higher altitudes and certain shade configurations (e.g., coffee-tree systems) are associated with lower CLR incidence

Shade effects on microclimate are complex and vary with altitude

Diurnal temperature range and humidity are key disease drivers

Shade system effects may mitigate or exacerbate CLR depending on environmental context
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Did not assess yield or socio-economic outcomes

Study area-specific results may not generalize across all agroecological zones

Other biotic and abiotic interactions not included in final model
NOTES:
Funded by BMZ and implemented under CCAFS

Collaborators include IITA, CIAT, CIRAD, and local farmers

Avelino and van Asten are long-standing contributors to coffee agroecology and systems disease management

TITLE: How future climatic uncertainty and biotic stressors might influence the sustainability of African vegetable production
AUTHORS: J.D.H. Keatinge, D.R. Ledesma, J.dâ€™A. Hughes, F.J.D. Keatinge, S. Hauser, P.C.S. Traore
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Pan-African (locations include Nigeria, Kenya, Mali, Tunisia, etc.)
METHODS: This review analyzes long-term temperature and rainfall data (1975â€“2014 and up to 100 years where available) across 13 African countries. It assesses climate trends and predicts future warming scenarios. The paper focuses on how increasing temperatures and climate variability will impact biotic stressors (pests, diseases, weeds) in African vegetable production systems, especially for smallholder farmers.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Temperature trends are projected for 25â€“100 years, and future risks to vegetable production are linked to increased pests, diseases, and weed pressures.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Climate change will exacerbate crop losses via biotic stressors

Rainfall trends remain highly variable and statistically insignificant

Localized responses neededâ€”climate trends vary by site

Temperature rise projected up to +6.87Â°C by 2115 in Tunisia; +1.11Â°C in Ibadan (IITA)

Highlights the need for temperature-adapted breeding and disease monitoring

Policy and funding should prioritize adaptive horticulture and farmer resilience
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Long-term rainfall data were scarce and trends were inconclusive

Highly variable local conditions limit regional generalizability

Did not include field-level pest or crop performance data
NOTES:
Strong integration of historical meteorological analysis

Supports Sustainable Development Goal 2 via improved horticulture

IITA and AVRDC data sources contributed to the climate analysis

TITLE: Habitat range shift and prediction of the potential future distribution of Ricinodendron heudelotii (Baill.) Heckel in Benin (West Africa)
AUTHORS: Guillaume Hounsou-Dindin, Rodrigue Idohou, Paterne Agre, Achille HounkpÃ¨vi, Aristide Cossi Adomou, Achille Ephrem Assogbadjo, Romain GlÃ¨lÃ¨ KakaÃ¯
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Benin (with emphasis on humid and sub-humid zones)
METHODS: Species distribution modeling (SDM) using an ensemble of four algorithms (RF, GBM, GLM, MAXENT) in the biomod2 framework was applied to current and projected climate and soil data (RCP 4.5 and 8.5 scenarios) to assess the present and future distribution of Ricinodendron heudelotii in Benin. Occurrence records were collected from field sampling and GBIF, and a predictive model was developed to identify suitable conservation and domestication zones.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central. RCP 4.5 and 8.5 scenarios are used to project shifts in suitable habitat zones through 2085.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Currently, 4.43% of Benin is highly suitable for R. heudelotii

Under both RCP scenarios, humid zone habitat suitability declines, while sub-humid zones gain importance

Model performance was very high (AUC = 0.994; TSS = 0.925)

Environmental variables (isothermality, rainfall, and soil sand content) are key predictors

Results inform national planning for climate-smart forest and agroforestry management
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
No modeling of biotic interactions, dispersal, or landscape connectivity

SDM limitations: does not predict actual presence, only suitability

No socioeconomic constraints modeled (e.g., land access, institutional barriers)
NOTES:
Recommends ex situ strategies (seedling propagation, assisted migration)

Important case study for climate-resilient agroforestry planning in West Africa

TITLE: Exâ€ante evaluation of promising soybean innovations for subâ€Saharan Africa
AUTHORS: Sika Gbegbelegbe, Arega Alene, Alpha Kamara, Keith Wiebe, Victor Manyong, Tahirou Abdoulaye, Petros Mkandawire
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (including country-level focus on Nigeria, Malawi, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Uganda, Benin, Ethiopia, Cameroon, CÃ´te d'Ivoire, Gabon, etc.)
IITA OFFICE: IITA Malawi, IITA Nigeria, IITA Tanzania
METHODS: The study uses a combination of crop modeling, hydrology modeling, and the IMPACT economic model to assess how soybean innovations (e.g., improved varieties, increased acreage, and expanded processing capacity) may reduce import dependency and enhance production in sub-Saharan Africa under future climate scenarios.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central to the modeling exercise. Yield impacts under RCP 8.5 are used to define risks and adaptation needs. The HadGEM2-ES model is used to simulate future drought-related stress.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
SSAâ€™s soybean import dependency is projected to worsen without intervention

Yield-enhancing tech alone insufficient; acreage and processing must also double

Combined innovations could make SSA self-sufficient in soybean by 2050

High-value recommendations for policy (e.g., subsidies, floor prices, contract farming)
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Does not include biotic stress effects

No income or poverty effects modeled (partial equilibrium model)

Assumes homogeneous technology uptake scenarios
NOTES:
Authors note need for CGE modeling to understand income and poverty outcomes

Modeling includes all major soybean-producing countries in SSA

Supported by CGIAR-PIM and Gates Foundation

TITLE: Rhizobium inoculant, nitrogen, and phosphorus improves photosynthesis and water use efficiency in soybean under drought stress
AUTHORS: Singh, M., Singh, V., Khera, D. et al.
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria
METHODS: Greenhouse pot experiments were conducted using soybean varieties, applying rhizobium inoculation, nitrogen, and phosphorus treatments. Photosynthesis and water use efficiency were measured under induced drought and well-watered conditions to assess physiological responses.
IMPACT AREAS: Climate adaptation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper investigates physiological mechanisms (photosynthesis, water use) through which soybean can become more drought-resilient, explicitly positioning the research within climate adaptation.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Inoculation with rhizobium plus nitrogen and phosphorus significantly enhanced photosynthesis and water use efficiency.

The combined treatment improved drought tolerance in soybean, suggesting a viable strategy to sustain productivity under climate stress.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Greenhouse study may not fully replicate field conditions; long-term effects not evaluated.
NOTES: Strong physiological focus with clear implications for adaptation but not broader food system themes.

TITLE: Forest governance and REDD+ in Central Africa: Towards a participatory model to increase stakeholder involvement in carbon markets
AUTHORS: Adrien N. Djomo, J. Andrew Grant, Celestine Fonyikeh-Bomboh Lucha, Julie Tchoko Gagoe, NoÃ«l H. Fonton, Neal Scott, Denis Jean Sonwa
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Central Africa (Congo Basin countries: Cameroon, DRC, Gabon, Republic of Congo, Central African Republic, Equatorial Guinea)
METHODS: The paper provides a literature-based and policy review of REDD+ initiatives in the Congo Basin, analyzing carbon stock monitoring, governance structures, stakeholder participation, and implementation challenges. It includes historical data synthesis, silvicultural system design, and proposed carbon accounting methodologies. The authors propose a participatory model for engaging local communities in carbon markets.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper centers on REDD+ as a climate mitigation mechanism and emphasizes the need for equitable governance, community incentives, and carbon accounting to effectively reduce deforestation in the Congo Basin.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
REDD+ must go beyond technical monitoring and involve participatory governance and local rights.

Accurate carbon accounting and forest reference levels are essential for access to carbon markets.

Community forestry, when legally recognized and properly funded, can yield carbon and livelihood benefits.

Forest degradation persists due to weak governance, insecure tenure, and lack of local incentives.

The authors propose a 30-year silvicultural system with regular inventories to improve forest management and carbon sequestration.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of funding mechanisms and secure land tenure in REDD+ implementation

Inadequate inclusion of bryophytes and non-tree biomass in current carbon accounting

Challenges with bureaucratic complexity and corruption in forest governance
NOTES:
The paper highlights IITAâ€™s and CIFORâ€™s roles in REDD+ policy development and research in Central Africa.

Authors stress that mitigation success requires legally binding tenure systems, simplified rules, and full local participation.

TITLE: Historical environmental change in Africa drives divergence and admixture of Aedes aegypti mosquitoes: a precursor to successful worldwide colonization?
AUTHORS: Kelly Louise Bennett, Fortunate Shija, Yvonne-Marie Linton, Gerald Misinzo, Martha Kaddumukasa, Rousseau Djouaka, Okorie Anyaele, Angela Harris, Seth Irish, Thaung Hlaing, Anil Prakash, Julius Lutwama, Catherine Walton
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West, Central, and East Africa (specifically Nigeria, Benin, Uganda, Tanzania, Kenya, and Ivory Coast)
METHODS: The study used genetic data (four nuclear and one mitochondrial marker) from Aedes aegypti mosquitoes collected across 19 sites in Africa and 17 global tropical locations. Through Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC), it tested demographic models to evaluate population structure, historical admixture, divergence, and colonization pathways. Findings were used to understand how environmental change shaped genetic structure and facilitated global invasion.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study links forest fragmentation and expansion due to Pleistocene and Holocene climate shifts to genetic divergence and admixture in Aedes aegypti. These evolutionary processes increased the mosquitoesâ€™ adaptive potential for human environments and global spread.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Historical climatic changes led to population divergence and secondary contact among Aedes aegypti populations in African forest refugia.

Genetic admixture during the Holocene facilitated the emergence of traits suitable for domestic environments.

Evidence supports a single out-of-Africa colonization event with decreased genetic diversity from Africa to the Americas and Asia.

The study highlights the importance of evolutionary history in vector control and disease prediction efforts.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Inability to definitively determine colonization route due to signal masking from admixture

ABC models are simplified and may not fully capture the complexity of evolutionary history

Variation in informativeness across genetic markers
NOTES:
The study improves understanding of vector adaptation and informs strategies for managing disease risk amid climate change.

It emphasizes the interplay between environmental change and biological invasion.

TITLE: A global meta-analysis of soil organic carbon in the Anthropocene
AUTHORS: Damien Beillouin, Marc Corbeels, Julien Demenois, David Berre, Annie Boyer, Abigail Fallot, FrÃ©dÃ©ric Feder, RÃ©mi Cardinael
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: N/a
METHODS: This second-order meta-analysis synthesized results from 230 first-order meta-analyses (25,000+ primary studies) to evaluate the effects of land-use change, land management, and climate change (direct and indirect) on soil organic carbon (SOC). Statistical comparisons were performed using both frequentist and Bayesian methods. The study assessed percent change in SOC by driver type and across land-use categories (croplands, forests, grasslands, wetlands, etc.).
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study explores both the direct (e.g., warming, COâ‚‚ enrichment) and indirect (e.g., wildfire, snow cover change) effects of climate change on SOC, finding that indirect effects may have greater SOC impacts than direct ones.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Land-use change and land management have a 7â€“10x larger impact on SOC than direct climate change effects.

Forest and wetland conversion to cropland leads to major SOC losses.

SOC gains can be achieved through agroforestry (+20â€“33%), biochar (+67%), and organic amendments (+29â€“34%).

SOC impacts vary significantly by land-use type; cropland has the highest potential for SOC gain.

Few studies focus on wetlands or indirect climate effectsâ€”highlighting major research gaps.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Some ecosystem types (e.g., wetlands) are underrepresented in meta-analyses

Most studies are short-term and may not capture long-term SOC dynamics

Results may not generalize well to all local contexts due to pedoclimatic variability
NOTES:
The article is a major contribution to the evidence base for global climate mitigation through soil carbon.

Supports policies promoting nature-based solutions, especially in agriculture and land management.

TITLE: Influences of climate variability on cocoa health and productivity in agroforestry systems in Ghana
AUTHORS: Bismark Kwesi Asitoakor, Richard Asare, Anders RÃ¦bild, Hans Peter Ravn, Vincent Yao Eziah, Kwadwo Owusu, Eric Opoku Mensah, Philippe Vaast
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana (Southern, Middle, and Northern cocoa belts)
METHODS: Longitudinal study (2016â€“2019) across 23 cocoa farms in Ghana's cocoa belts. Monitored 460 cocoa trees for canopy health, flowering, pest/disease damage, and yield. Used rainfall gauges, temperature loggers, and structured observations. Employed linear mixed models to assess effects of climatic factors, shade tree characteristics, and farm practices on productivity.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Study focuses on how climate gradients (rainfall and temperature) affect cocoa health and productivity. High rainfall enhances canopy health and flowering; higher temperatures increase pest incidence. Findings support climate-smart shading and management practices.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Cocoa productivity was highest in the wetter southern cocoa belt and lowest in the drier north. 

Black pod disease was most common in humid areas, while mirids and shield bugs were more prevalent in dry zones. 

Larger crown area of shade trees improved canopy health but also increased black pod risk. 

Distance from shade trees had mixed effects on flowering and pest damage. 

Farm management practices significantly influenced outcomes.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: No soil moisture data collected; complex interactions between shade trees, pests, and climate require further study; results may not fully generalize outside Ghana.
NOTES: Highlights role of climate-smart agroforestry and pruning practices. Recommends careful selection of shade tree species to balance benefits and risks.

TITLE: Gender in climate change, agriculture, and natural resource policies: insights from East Africa
AUTHORS: Edidah L. Ampaire, Mariola Acosta, Sofia Huyer, Ritah Kigonya, Perez Muchunguzi, Rebecca Muna, Laurence Jassogne
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Uganda and Tanzania
METHODS: Content analysis of 155 agricultural and natural resource policies, development plans, and action plans from national, district, and sub-county/ward levels. Gender budgeting review based on district and sub-county budgets from 2012â€“2016. Evaluation based on degree of gender integration, budget allocations, and stakeholder workshops.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on institutional and policy readiness for gender-sensitive climate action. Reviews how national and sub-national policies integrate gender to support climate change resilience and adaptation in agriculture and natural resources.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Gender integration in policies has improved since the 1990s, but major gaps remain in execution, budgeting, and structural analysis. 

Most policies treat gender as a 'womenâ€™s issue' and fail to address menâ€™s vulnerabilities or intersectionality.

Budget allocations to gender are minimal and inconsistent, with little funding reaching structural interventions. 

Stakeholders reported capacity gaps and low technical ability to apply gender-responsive budgeting.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Focuses only on two countries and four years of budget data. Analysis based on publicly accessible policy documents and stakeholder feedback.
NOTES: The study is part of the CGIAR CCAFS program. It recommends clearer national frameworks for gender mainstreaming and budget tracking. Includes a 5-point grading rubric for gender integration in policy documents and a proposal for harmonized gender strategies across levels of governance.

TITLE: Gender and impact of climate change adaptation on soybean farmersâ€™ revenue in rural Togo, West Africa
AUTHORS: Essossinam Ali, NadÃ¨ge Essossolim Awade, Tahirou Abdoulaye
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Central Region, Togo (Tchamba, Sotouboua, and Blitta districts)
METHODS: Cross-sectional survey of 500 soybean farmers analyzed using endogenous switching regression. Evaluated gender-differentiated impacts of climate change adaptation on farm revenue, considering access to extension, credit, training, and land ownership.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Addresses climate change adaptation through strategies like crop rotation, seed selection, adjusted sowing, and agroforestry. Assesses impacts of these strategies under rising temperature and declining rainfall patterns in Togo.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Only 40.37% of women adopted climate adaptation strategies compared to 59.62% of men. 

Women who adapted had higher soybean revenue than men, but women who did not adapt had significantly lower revenue. 

Key drivers of adaptation included access to extension, credit, education, and farmer-based organizations. 

Non-adaptation led to a 0.268% decrease in revenue. 

Use of crop rotation and access to credit significantly increased revenue.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Cross-sectional data may not capture seasonal effects; limited to three districts; does not account for long-term effects of adaptation choices.
NOTES: Study recommends gender mainstreaming in climate adaptation policies. Supported by AGRA, IDRC, CORAF/WECARD, and IITA.

TITLE: Gender-responsive approaches to rapid climate warming among smallholder farmers
AUTHORS: Abraham Akyala, Ayansina Ayanlade, Olajumoke Adeyeye, Isaac A. Oluwatimilehin
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nasarawa State, Nigeria
METHODS: Used 36-year temperature dataset (1984â€“2020) from Nigeria Meteorological Agency and primary survey of 52 purposively selected smallholder farmers (26 men, 26 women) using semi-structured questionnaires and in-depth interviews. Data analyzed with Mann-Kendall trend test, descriptive stats, and thematic coding of interview data.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on rapid climate warming trends and gender-differentiated perceptions and responses among smallholder farmers. Uses observed temperature trends and self-reported adaptation strategies to explore differential vulnerability and resilience.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Statistical trend in increasing max temperature confirmed rapid warming. 

Women adopted traditional irrigation (66%) and cover cropping (75%) more often; men favored sustainable forestry (74%). 

Gender norms, land rights, and access to resources significantly shaped adaptation strategies. 

Education, income, and institutional support were all impacted by warming. 

Womenâ€™s proximity to natural resources increased climate awareness, but they faced constraints in land access and support services.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Small sample size; localized to Nasarawa State; relies on self-reported adaptation behavior and perception data.
NOTES: Recommends integrating gender into climate finance and adaptive capacity-building. Gender-informed policies should support womenâ€™s knowledge, access, and innovation.

TITLE: A Global Assessment of the State of Plant Health
AUTHORS: Serge Savary et al., Global Plant Health Assessment Project (GPHA)
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with specific focus on regions including Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and others
IITA OFFICE: IITA Nigeria, IITA Kenya, IITA Cameroon, IITA Tanzania
METHODS: Collaborative expert-driven global assessment of plant health across 33 plant system Ã— ecoregion combinations. Synthesized evidence from literature and expert reports using a structured framework based on the Millennium Ecosystem Assessment. Included evaluations of provisioning, regulating, and cultural ecosystem services and their trends.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Extensively addresses the impact of climate change on plant health, emphasizing interactions between climate variability, pathogen invasions, and phytobiome dynamics in agrosystems and natural ecosystems.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Most plant systems assessed are in fair or poor health, with many in decline. 

Major drivers include climate change, human activity, and pathogen invasions. 

The state of plant health is especially poor in Sub-Saharan Africa, contributing to food insecurity and environmental degradation. 

The report advocates for improved data, collective action, and resilience strategies such as genetic diversity and ecosystem-based management.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Relies on expert assessments due to lack of quantitative global data; plant health considered mainly through lens of infectious disease; ecological complexity and phytobiome dynamics not fully integrated.
NOTES: Coordinated by the International Society for Plant Pathology and supported by IITA and global institutions; structured around the Millennium Ecosystem Assessment framework; emphasizes need for multidisciplinary research and policy engagement.

TITLE: Indigenous African Orphan Legumes: Potential for Food and Nutrition Security in SSA
AUTHORS: Michael Abberton, Rajneesh Paliwal, Benjamin Faloye, Tchamba Marimagne, Azeez Moriam, Olaniyi Oyatomi
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa
METHODS: Review of literature on African orphan legumes with emphasis on genetic resources, breeding methods, and nutritional and agronomic value.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper emphasizes the importance of climate adaptation through crop diversification and the development of drought-tolerant legumes
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Orphan legumes offer nutritional and ecological benefits

GRC-IITA conserves critical germplasm

genomic tools hold promise for improvement

however, underinvestment and lack of breeding research remain constraints
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Limited research and breeding programs; constraints like low yield, antinutritional factors, and poor market access hinder adoption.
NOTES: Includes quantitative BNF data and lists constraints and potential of each legume crop.

TITLE: Global agricultural intensification during climate change: a role for genomics
AUTHORS: Michael Abberton, Jacqueline Batley, Alison Bentley, John Bryant, Hongwei Cai, James Cockram, Antonio Costa de Oliveira, Leland J. Cseke, et al.
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global (broad scope; includes examples from Asia, Africa, Europe)
IITA OFFICE: Yes â€“ IITA affiliation noted with lead author
METHODS: This is a narrative literature review synthesizing current knowledge on genomic-assisted breeding and its role in increasing food production resilience to climate change. It presents a high-level synthesis of genomics applications and stresses the urgency of investment in genomic tools to address crop vulnerabilities globally.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is framed as a major driver necessitating a â€œsecond Green Revolution.â€ The paper emphasizes rising global temperatures, altered rainfall, increased pests, and greater yield instability. Genomics is presented as a critical tool for building resilience through new breeding strategies.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Climate stressors threaten current food production models.

Genomic-assisted breeding is essential for climate-resilient crops.

A shift away from high-input agriculture is needed.

Minor/orphan crops should receive more attention.

Genomic tools can expand crop diversity, improve stability, and promote food security.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Major investments are needed to realize genomic potential.

Breeding gains may take decades to fully materialize.

Limited progress in applying genomics to orphan crops and marginalized regions.

TITLE: Biological control of aflatoxins in Africa: current status and potential challenges in the face of climate change
AUTHORS: R. Bandyopadhyay, A. Ortega-Beltran, A. Akande, C. Mutegi, J. Atehnkeng, L. Kaptoge, A.L. Senghor, B.N. Adhikari, P.J. Cotty
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (with specific references to Nigeria, Kenya, Senegal, The Gambia, Ghana, Zambia, Malawi, Tanzania, Uganda, Mozambique, and Burkina Faso)
IITA OFFICE: IITA Nigeria, IITA Kenya, IITA Malawi
METHODS: This paper synthesizes policy, technical, and institutional experiences from the development and scaling of Aflasafe in Africa. It includes discussions of efficacy data, regulatory processes, and implementation models, especially in response to climate change risks.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Discusses increasing aflatoxin risk due to climate change and how Aflasafe technology can help mitigate these impacts.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Aflasafe has demonstrated over 80% reduction in aflatoxins.

Local production, public-private partnerships, and premium markets are key for scaling.

Native atoxigenic fungi are essential for ecological safety and effectiveness.

Climate change is increasing the urgency for aflatoxin management.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Weak biopesticide registration frameworks

Market and consumer awareness challenges

Potential distribution cost burdens

Regulatory unfamiliarity with microbial biocontrols
NOTES:
Emphasis on scaling through both public health policy and private sector engagement.

The IITA Aflasafe plant in Ibadan, Nigeria, is a key manufacturing and dissemination hub.

TITLE: Comparative Multi-Criteria Assessment of Climate Policies and Sustainable Development Strategies in Cameroon: Towards a GIS Decision-Support Tool for the Design of an Optimal REDD+ Strategy
AUTHORS: Anderson Gwanyebit Kehbila, Dieudonne Alemagi, Peter Akong Minang
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Cameroon (national-level, Congo Basin relevance)
METHODS: This study uses a multi-criteria analysis framework to evaluate five key national policies in Cameroon for their compatibility with REDD+ objectives. The authors develop and propose a GIS-based decision-support system (GIS-REDD+DSS) to strengthen adaptive REDD+ planning. Policies assessed include PRSP, GESP, FESP, Energy Policy, and the First National Communication on Climate Change. Criteria were developed from global REDD+ and sustainable development frameworks.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Central to the study. It assesses national policy coherence with REDD+ goals and presents an adaptive, GIS-based decision framework for carbon emissions reductions, forest protection, and land-use planning.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Most policies (PRSP, FESP, Energy Policy, etc.) lack clear REDD+ targets or safeguards

PRSP and GESP align with poverty and sustainable development but lack climate planning integration

FNCCC1 includes mitigation/adaptation but lacks operationalization

GIS-DSS model includes databases, valuation tools, and a virtual REDD+ marketplace

Urges policy harmonization across sectors for effective REDD+ rollout in Cameroon and Congo Basin countries
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Subjectivity in interpreting policies

Lack of concrete implementation measures across assessed policies

DSS effectiveness depends on national capacity and data availability
NOTES:
Developed under the CGIAR â€œReducing Emissions from All Land Uses (REALU)â€ project

Relevance extends to other Congo Basin countries with similar socio-ecological settings

Offers criteria to guide â€œREDD+ safeguard integrationâ€ at national and regional levels

TITLE: A stepwise approach to facilitate adoption of climate smart practices for smallholder coffee production in Uganda
AUTHORS: Mukasa, D., Rusinamhodzi, L., van Asten, Piet. J. A., Amwonya, D., Sseguya, H., Okiror, F. A., … Jassogne, L.
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Uganda
METHODS: The details of study design are described elaborated by the theoretical basis for the Stepwise approach. The study considered action research and case study research design. It consisted of three main components that helped to shape the Stepwise logic: (a) co-design and co-development, (b) experiential learning, (c) farmers’ feedback, and (d) Hypothesis needed for field testing to ascertain its validity and practicality. The combination of these brought about the idea of breaking down the GAPs packages into smaller packages that could be implemented in phases. The process of development of the approach, tools, experiential learning, farmers’ feedback, assumptions, and data underpinning Stepwise approach.
ABSTRACT: Adoption of Climate-smart Agricultural Practices (CSA) enhances crop productivity and livelihoods. Smallholder coffee farmers in Uganda receive training on good agricultural practices (GAPs) through extension programs, but adoption remains low. Adopting all ‘best practices’ at once may be unrealistic for resource-limited, risk-averse smallholders. This study tested a Stepwise approach, breaking GAPs into manageable, incremental investments. Field trials were conducted in Luweero (Central) and Sironko (East) districts of Uganda, with 16 demonstration sites using randomized treatments and the control (farmer practice), divided into four steps. Each site served 25–30 farmers through experiential learning. A co-design method ensured farmer involvement in the design, development, and testing for sustainability. Results from two harvests (2018–2019) showed significant cumulative yield gains over the control. Arabica yields increased by 31%, 43%, 54% and 65% across Steps 1-4,. Robusta showed gains of 7%, 22%, 23% and 39% respectively. Marginal rate of returns (MRR) was relatively high for Step 1 (563%), 2 (169%) and 4 (122%) for Robusta coffee , and 221%, 217% and 485% for Step 2, 3 and 4 for Arabica coffee respectively. The Stepwise approach demonstrated improved yield gains and increased farmer income.
KEYWORDS: Coffee, productivity, GAPs, MRR, Stepwise approach, Uganda
